title: EGR flow insufficient detected
description: The ECU detected less exhaust gas recirculation flow than expected.
probable_causes: Carbon buildup in the EGR valve or EGR cooler passages
Faulty EGR valve position feedback
Blocked intake/EGR path on high-mileage OM651 engines
Vacuum or actuator control issue, depending on variant
step_by_step_fix: Confirm the code with a diagnostic scanner and record freeze-frame data.
Inspect EGR wiring, connector pins, and vacuum/control lines where fitted.
Check live data for commanded versus actual EGR position and air mass deviation.
Remove and inspect the EGR valve and accessible EGR passages for carbon restriction.
Clean or replace the EGR valve/cooler components if movement or flow is restricted.
Clear codes and road-test while monitoring EGR and MAF live data.
safety_warning: Work only on a cool engine and avoid inhaling soot deposits. Disconnect the battery if removing electrical actuators.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on common OM651 diagnostic patterns

title: Catalyst system efficiency below threshold
description: The ECU determined that catalyst efficiency is lower than expected on bank 1.
probable_causes: Aging or damaged catalytic converter
Exhaust leak before or near the oxygen sensors
Faulty downstream oxygen sensor
Misfires or rich running that damaged the catalyst
Aftermarket catalyst with low efficiency
symptoms: Possible fuel smell or poor fuel economy
step_by_step_fix: Check for other codes first, especially misfire, fuel trim, or oxygen sensor codes.
Inspect the exhaust for leaks before the catalyst and around sensor bungs.
Review upstream and downstream oxygen sensor waveforms; downstream should be steadier than upstream.
Verify fuel trims and fix rich/lean or misfire issues before replacing the catalyst.
If sensor data and exhaust integrity confirm low efficiency, replace the catalyst with an OE-quality part.
Clear the code and complete the catalyst monitor drive cycle.
safety_warning: Exhaust components get extremely hot. Let the vehicle cool fully and support it securely before inspection.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on generic OBD-II catalyst diagnostics

title: Mass air flow sensor range/performance
description: Measured air mass is outside the expected range for the current operating conditions.
probable_causes: Dirty or faulty MAF sensor
Air leak after the MAF sensor
EGR flow deviation affecting air mass readings
Restricted air filter or intake tract
Boost leak on turbocharged engines
step_by_step_fix: Inspect the air filter, airbox sealing, and intake hoses after the MAF.
Check MAF live data at idle and under light load against expected values.
Inspect boost hoses and intercooler connections for oil mist or splits.
Check EGR operation because stuck EGR can skew expected air mass on OM651.
Clean only with proper MAF cleaner or replace the MAF if readings remain implausible.
Clear codes and road-test while logging MAF, boost, and EGR values.
safety_warning: Keep hands and tools away from moving belts and fans while checking live data with the engine running.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on Mercedes diesel air-path diagnostics

title: Turbocharger underboost
description: Boost pressure is lower than the ECU target under load.
probable_causes: Split boost hose or loose intercooler connection
Vacuum/control fault for turbo actuator where applicable
Sticking turbo actuator or vane mechanism
Blocked DPF causing abnormal exhaust backpressure
Faulty boost pressure sensor
step_by_step_fix: Inspect charge-air hoses from turbo to intercooler and intake for splits or oil stains.
Check boost pressure requested versus actual during a controlled road test.
Inspect vacuum lines, pressure converter, and turbo actuator movement where fitted.
Check boost/MAP sensor readings and connector condition.
Evaluate DPF differential pressure if underboost appears with exhaust restriction symptoms.
Repair leaks or actuator faults, clear codes, and retest under load.
safety_warning: Do not road-test aggressively on public roads. Turbo and exhaust parts can cause burns after running.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on OM651 boost system patterns

title: DPF differential pressure sensor range/performance
description: The DPF pressure sensor signal is implausible or out of expected range.
probable_causes: Faulty differential pressure sensor
Blocked, cracked, or swapped pressure hoses
Soot-loaded DPF
Wiring or connector fault
Exhaust leak affecting pressure reading
step_by_step_fix: Inspect both DPF pressure hoses for cracks, blockage, melting, or reversed connection.
Read differential pressure at key-on/engine-off; it should be near zero.
Check differential pressure at idle and under raised RPM to assess soot loading.
Verify sensor power, ground, and signal integrity.
Replace the sensor or hoses if readings are implausible before condemning the DPF.
After repair, reset learned values if required and confirm regeneration readiness.
safety_warning: DPF regeneration creates very high exhaust temperatures. Do not perform forced regeneration near flammable materials.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on Mercedes DPF diagnostics

title: DPF soot accumulation
description: The ECU estimates excessive soot load in the diesel particulate filter.
probable_causes: Repeated interrupted regenerations
Faulty DPF differential pressure sensor or hoses
Thermostat issue preventing correct regeneration temperature
Injector or boost issue producing excess soot
Ash/soot-loaded DPF
step_by_step_fix: Check for related codes before attempting regeneration.
Verify coolant temperature reaches normal operating range.
Check differential pressure sensor plausibility and hose condition.
Inspect for boost leaks, injector issues, or EGR problems that increase soot.
If soot load is within safe limits, perform a service regeneration with proper equipment.
If ash load is high or pressure remains excessive, remove and professionally clean or replace the DPF.
safety_warning: Forced regeneration can overheat exhaust components. Use professional equipment and keep the vehicle in a safe ventilated area.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on Mercedes diesel aftertreatment diagnostics

title: Crankshaft/camshaft correlation
description: The relationship between crankshaft and camshaft position signals is outside the expected range.
probable_causes: Timing chain stretch or timing deviation
Faulty camshaft or crankshaft position sensor
Incorrect timing after repair
Oil pressure or cam adjuster issue where applicable
Wiring or connector fault
step_by_step_fix: Do not keep driving if chain rattle or severe running issues are present.
Check for sensor wiring damage and confirm crank/cam signal quality.
Compare actual cam/crank correlation values with diagnostic equipment.
Inspect timing chain noise, tensioner condition, and service history.
If timing deviation is confirmed, inspect/replace timing chain components before engine damage occurs.
Clear codes and verify correlation values after repair.
safety_warning: Incorrect timing repair can cause major engine damage. Do not crank repeatedly if mechanical timing is suspect.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on OM651 timing-chain symptom patterns

title: Intake manifold runner performance
description: The intake runner/swirl flap system is not performing as expected.
probable_causes: Swirl flap mechanism sticking from carbon/oil deposits
Faulty intake runner actuator
Broken linkage or worn flap mechanism
Wiring or connector fault
Intake manifold internal wear
step_by_step_fix: Inspect actuator connector and wiring.
Command the intake runner actuator with a scanner if supported.
Check linkage movement and look for broken or disconnected parts.
Inspect intake for heavy carbon contamination.
Clean or replace affected intake runner components/manifold as needed.
Clear adaptations or perform actuator relearn where required.
safety_warning: Avoid dropping debris into the intake ports. Cover exposed openings during manifold work.
sources: Internal seed knowledge based on Mercedes diesel intake runner diagnostics
